cards.text: An {{c1::in}} parameter is read from but not written to. An {{c2::out}} parameter is written to for the purpose of returning data. An {{c3::in-out}} parameter is both read and written to.

cards.front: What parameter passing method should be used for an 'in' parameter?
cards.back: For fundamental types: pass by value. For class types: pass by const reference. This prevents modification and avoids unnecessary copies.

cards.front: What parameter passing method should be used for an 'out' parameter?
cards.back: Pass by non-const reference (or non-const pointer if nullptr is meaningful). This allows the function to modify the parameter to return a value.

cards.question: Identify the in, out, and in-out parameters:
cards.code:
void func(int x, int& result, int& accumulator) {
result = x * 2; // out
accumulator += x; // in-out
}
cards.answer: x is 'in' (only read), result is 'out' (only written), accumulator is 'in-out' (read and written).
cards.explanation: The parameter usage determines its category: read-only (in), write-only (out), or both (in-out).

Choose parameter type based on usage:

IN parameters:
  • Fundamental types: pass by value
  • Class types: pass by const reference
OUT parameters:
  • Pass by reference (or pointer if optional)
IN-OUT parameters:
  • Pass by reference
examples: void process(int x, const std::string& s);  // in: value, const ref
void getSinCos(double angle, double& s, double& c);  // in, out, out

cards.text: For fundamental types, pass {{c1::in}} parameters by {{c2::value}}. For class types, pass by {{c3::const reference}}.
{{c1::Out}} parameters are used to return data from a function and should be passed by {{c2::non-const reference}} or {{c3::pointer}}.
